title: Hemifacial Microsomia / Goldenhar Spectrum

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Second most common craniofacial anomaly**

• 1/3,500-5,000 births
• Spectrum of severity
• Usually sporadic
• First and second branchial arch derivatives affected

**Terminology:**
• Hemifacial microsomia (HFM)
• Craniofacial microsomia
• Oculoauriculovertebral spectrum (OAVS)
• Goldenhar syndrome (with epibulbar dermoid + vertebral)

sections.title: Clinical Features
sections.content:
**Mandible:**
• Hypoplasia (variable severity)
• Absent condyle/ramus in severe cases
• Facial asymmetry

**Ear:**
• Microtia spectrum
• Preauricular tags
• Aural atresia
• Conductive hearing loss

**Soft Tissue:**
• Facial nerve weakness
• Macrostomia (lateral oral cleft)
• Parotid hypoplasia
• Masticatory muscle hypoplasia

**Eye:**
• Epibulbar dermoid (Goldenhar)
• Upper lid coloboma

**Spine (Goldenhar):**
• Hemivertebrae
• Cervical fusion

sections.title: Classification
sections.content:
**OMENS Classification:**
• **O**rbit
• **M**andible (I-III)
• **E**ar
• **N**erve (facial)
• **S**oft tissue

**Pruzansky/Kaban Mandible:**

**Type I:**
• Small but normal shape
• All components present

**Type IIA:**
• Abnormal shape
• Functional TMJ

**Type IIB:**
• Abnormal shape
• Non-functional TMJ

**Type III:**
• Absent ramus/condyle/TMJ

sections.title: Management
sections.content:
**Timing Principles:**
• Address functional issues first (airway, hearing)
• Bony surgery after growth assessment
• Soft tissue follows bone

**Hearing:**
• Early amplification
• BAHA if canal atresia

**Mandible:**
• Type I/IIA: May observe
• Type IIB/III: Distraction osteogenesis or costochondral graft
• Orthognathic surgery at skeletal maturity

**Ear:**
• Microtia reconstruction ~age 6-10
• Rib cartilage framework
• Or prosthetic ear

**Soft Tissue:**
• Fat grafting
• Free tissue transfer for severe cases

keyTakeaways: Second most common craniofacial anomaly after cleft
Spectrum of severity - use OMENS classification
Screen for cervical spine anomalies (intubation risk)
Mandible treatment depends on severity type

reviewQuestions.question: A child with hemifacial microsomia is scheduled for surgery. What screening is important before intubation?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Hemifacial microsomia (especially Goldenhar variant) is associated with cervical spine anomalies including hemivertebrae and fusions. Cervical spine imaging is important before intubation to assess for instability and plan airway management.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected hemifacial microsomia?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in hemifacial microsomia. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of hemifacial microsomia. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing hemifacial microsomia, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
